text: Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) have emerged as highly effective architectures for modeling structured data within recommender systems. However, real-world recommendation scenarios are inherently complex, often involving heterogeneous relationships—such as social influences between users or semantic dependencies between items—that offer rich potential for refining user preference learning. In this paper, we investigate the challenge of leveraging heterogeneous graph-enhanced relational learning for recommendation. Given the recent success of self-supervised techniques, we introduce Heterogeneous Graph Contrastive Learning (HGCL), a framework designed to bridge heterogeneous relational semantics with user-item interaction modeling. HGCL employs contrastive learning to facilitate robust knowledge transfer across diverse graph views. Recognizing that the impact of side information is not uniform, we further enhance our framework with meta-networks that enable personalized knowledge transformation through adaptive contrastive augmentation. Extensive experiments on three real-world datasets confirm that HGCL significantly outperforms state-of-the-art recommendation methods. Detailed ablation studies further validate that the individual components of HGCL are essential to its performance gains. The implementation of our model is available at https://github.com/HKUDS/HGCL.
